title: Rich document — all block types example

content.text: Rich document — all block types example

item.question: What is a variable?
item.answer: A **name** bound to a value in memory via assignment.

item.scenario: A teammate assigns `total = items or []` when `items` may legitimately be an empty list.
item.question: What goes wrong when `items` is `[]`?
item.answer: `or` treats `[]` as falsy, so `total` becomes `[]` from the right-hand side—but if they meant "default when None", they should use `items if items is not None else []`.

item.title: Grounding support replies
item.context: A SaaS team drafts ticket replies with an LLM.
item.problem: Early pilots cited policy sections that did not exist.
item.solution: Paste the KB article into the prompt and require quote-backed answers; agents approve before send.
item.outcome: Wrong-policy citations dropped; handle time stayed flat because agents still edited tone.

item.title: Build a typed config loader
item.description: Load a JSON config file, validate required keys, and expose typed accessors.

item.question: Which operator tests object identity?
item.explanation: `is` checks whether two names refer to the same object; `==` checks value equality.

item.title: Python data model
item.author: Python Software Foundation
item.description: Official reference for objects, names, and references.

item.title: Mutable default arguments
item.prefer: def append_item(x, bucket=None): bucket = bucket if bucket is not None else []
item.why: Default list is created once at definition time and shared across calls.
item.example: Two calls without `bucket` mutate the same list.

entries.label: Falsy values
entries.note: Empty containers are falsy; be careful with `or` defaults.

entries.label: Safe default
entries.code:
value = cfg.get('k')
result = value if value is not None else default

item.definition: Whether a value is treated as true or false in a boolean context.

item.title: Name binding
item.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  A["name"] --> B["references object in memory"]
  C["reassignment"] --> D["name points at a new object"]
item.caption: Variables are names; assignment rebinds the name.
